subject: Physics
chapter_name: The kinetic particle model of matter

note_data.title: The kinetic particle model of matter
keyPoints: All matter is made up of particles that are in **constant random motion**
The **kinetic energy** of particles increases with temperature
In gases, particles are **far apart** and move freely in all directions
In liquids, particles are **close together** but can move past each other
In solids, particles are **close together** and vibrate about fixed positions
**Gas pressure** is caused by particles colliding with container walls
At higher temperatures, gas particles move **faster** and collide more frequently
**Brownian motion** provides evidence for the kinetic particle model
The **absolute zero** temperature (-273°C) is when particle motion would stop completely

definitions.definition: A model that describes matter as being made up of particles in constant random motion
The random movement of particles suspended in a fluid caused by collisions with fast-moving molecules
The lowest possible temperature (-273°C or 0K) at which particle motion would cease
The force per unit area exerted by gas particles colliding with the walls of their container

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give a detailed account of the particle arrangement and movement

modelAnswer.question: Explain why the pressure of a gas in a sealed container increases when the temperature is raised. [3 marks]
modelAnswer.answer: When temperature increases, the kinetic energy of gas particles increases. This causes the particles to move faster. The faster-moving particles collide with the container walls more frequently and with greater force, resulting in increased pressure.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Temperature increase causes kinetic energy to increase
Mark 3: More frequent/harder collisions with walls increase pressure

commonMistakes: Never say particles 'stop moving' at absolute zero — say motion would cease or become minimal
Students often confuse particle spacing in liquids and gases — liquids have particles close together
Never say gas particles are 'packed closely' — they are far apart with large spaces between them
Don't say pressure is caused by particle expansion — it's caused by collisions with container walls
Students forget that particles are always moving, even in solids they vibrate
examinerTips: This topic appears in almost every Paper 2, often linked to gas laws or states of matter
Always mention 'random motion' when describing particle movement
Draw particle diagrams showing relative spacing and movement for different states
Use terms like 'kinetic energy' rather than just 'energy' for precision
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Particle arrangement diagrams showing: solids (particles close, vibrating in fixed positions), liquids (particles close, sliding past each other), gases (particles far apart, moving freely in all directions with motion arrows)
